description: An edge or point is 'shoru' when it does not cut or pierce readily. The adjective may extend to attention or thought whose acuity has faded. Low illumination belongs to 'nuelo' (dark), so 'shoru' does not inherit the English light-sense of dull.
articulatory_notes: The tongue blade approaches the postalveolar area for voiceless 'sh', and the lips round into mid-back 'o'. A tap or short trill begins the second syllable before rounded high-back 'u'. Stress falls on 'sho'.
usage_notes: The phrase 'shoru kiru' means a dull chisel. Its physical contrast is 'tiso' (sharp). Figurative use stays with reduced acuity rather than dim light or a judgement that something is boring.

semantic_domains.cognition: attention or thought with reduced acuity
semantic_domains.physical: an edge or point that no longer cuts or pierces readily
